coredata.dc:title: Integrate nanoscale assembly and plasmonic resonance to enhance photoluminescence of cellulose nanocrystals for optical information hiding and reading
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Shuyu
Liu, Siyuan
Xiao, Bo
Liu, Liu
Wan, Xinjie
Gong, Yanbin
Wei, Siqi
Luo, Chenglong
Gan, Lin
Huang, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Assembling cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) can induce photonic emission. It is free of photobleaching and aggregation-induced quenching, which has great potential in the application of information security. However, the emission suffers a low emission quantum yield (EQY), and the assembly mediate (solvents) can affect the assembly or the emission. Herein, we established a strategy of nanoscale assembly to integrate emission enhancement methods and improve the assembly induced emission. Via controlling the CNC assembly in the nanoscale space of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), the EQY of CNC array primarily increased by 50 %. When carbon nanodots were further incorporated in MOF, their plasmonic resonance could enhance the emission coupling rate and offset the loss in emission intensity from the extinction of MOF particles, leading to a 6.9-time increase in EQY (to 64.84 %). Such a high EQY from two emission enhancement mechanisms make this nanomaterial able to hide and present photonic information effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystal
Emission
Enhancement
Plasmonic resonance
Hiding optical information

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology of CNC, CND, ZIF-8@CNC and CND@ZIF-8@CNC
Assemble cellulose nanocrystals in the nanoscale space of metal-organic frameworks
Enhance the photonic emission of CNC assembly

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51973175 ), the Project for Chongqing University Innovation Research Group of Chongqing Education Committee ( CXQT19008 ), the Chongqing Talent Plan for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Demonstration Team ( CQYC201903243 ), and the S&T Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Bishan District in Chongqing ( Bskj20200011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pathway followed to fabricate CND@ZIF-8@CNC: (i) Modify ZIF-8 onto the surface of CNC and decrease the surface charge of CNC; (ii) Nanoscale assembly of CNC, and form nanoscale environment of ZIF-8; and (iii) Add carbon nanodots into the ZIF-8@CNC system.
a) AFM image, b) length distribution and c) diameter distribution of CNC; d) AFM image, e) length distribution and f) diameter distribution of ZIF-8@CNC (ω ZIF-8 = 0.0416).
ζ-potential of CNC, CND@ZIF-8@CNC, ZIF-8@CNC, CND, and ZIF-8.

a) XRD patterns of CNC, ZIF-8@CNC, ZIF-8, and simulated ZIF-8. b) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8@CNC with different content of ZIF-8. c) N 1s XPS analysis of ZIF-8, ZIF-8@CNC and CNC. d) O 1s XPS analysis of CNC, and ZIF-8@CNC, and ZIF-8. e) The table of the contents of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), hydrogen (H), and sulphur (S) elements in the CNC and ZIF-8@CNC.

a) Photoluminescence (PL) Intensity of ZIF-8@CNC, CNC and ZIF-8. b) PL Intensity of CND@ZIF-8@CNC, CND@CNC and CND@ZIF-8. c) Photoluminescence enhancement ratio (PLER) of CNC, ZIF-8@CNC, CND@ZIF-8@CNC, CND@CNC and ZIF-8.

a) Pathway followed to hide information using the invisible ink of 5 wt% CND@ZIF-8@CNC suspension, and images of a document with printing words from black inks and handwriting words from CND@ZIF-8@CNC ink under natural light and 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light. b) Some pictures drawn with the invisible ink of 5 wt% CND@ZIF-8@CNC suspension.

PL intensity of different carbon nanodots in CND@ZIF-8@CNC: a) CND1, b) CND2, c) CND3, d) CND4, and e) CND5. f) Maximal photoluminescence intensity of different CNDs. g) The table of the maximal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of different CNDs and CNC.

head.ce:title: Integrate nanoscale assembly and plasmonic resonance to enhance photoluminescence of cellulose nanocrystals for optical information hiding and reading

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Engineering Research Center of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering of Xinjiang Bintuan, Shihezi University, Shihezi 832003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Assembling cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) can induce photonic emission. It is free of photobleaching and aggregation-induced quenching, which has great potential in the application of information security. However, the emission suffers a low emission quantum yield (EQY), and the assembly mediate (solvents) can affect the assembly or the emission. Herein, we established a strategy of nanoscale assembly to integrate emission enhancement methods and improve the assembly induced emission. Via controlling the CNC assembly in the nanoscale space of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), the EQY of CNC array primarily increased by 50 %. When carbon nanodots were further incorporated in MOF, their plasmonic resonance could enhance the emission coupling rate and offset the loss in emission intensity from the extinction of MOF particles, leading to a 6.9-time increase in EQY (to 64.84 %). Such a high EQY from two emission enhancement mechanisms make this nanomaterial able to hide and present photonic information effectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) can induce structural colors through self-assembly, which belongs to physical colors that generated from the light interference, scattering, or diffraction in micro/nanostructure (Kim, Lee, Lee, & Kim, 2019; Meng et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Hail, Schnoering, Damak, Poulikakos, & Eghlidi, 2020; Sun et al., 2020). The achiral assembly of CNCs was recently reported to be able to present a noniridescent structural color and further cause blue emission without any fluorophores (Gan et al., 2019). That emission was caused by electron transition from virtual states to the base state, making the emission free of photobleaching (Nie & Emery, 1997; Choi, Kim, Oh, & Nam, 2019; Prinz, Matkovic, Pesic, Gajic, & Bald, 2016; Hennig, Monkemoller, Boger, Muller, & Huser, 2015). However, the emission quantum yield (EQY) based on this transition is rather low (Chen, Han, & Wu, 1993; Gan et al., 2015), compared to the solid-state fluorescent materials.

Fortunately, many works have been reported to enhance photoluminescence and their optical signals, especially based on plasmonic resonance (Wu, Zhu, Dufresne, & Lin, 2019, 2019b). For example, carbon nanodots (CNDs) were reported to be used as surface plasmon to enhance the quantum efficiency of quantum dots (Junka, Guo, Filpponen, Laine, & Rojas, 2014; Guo, Shan, Liu, Lou, & Shen, 2015). However, this method was seldom applied in the solid-state emission, because the surface plasmons could not have a direct physical interaction with the luminous substrate (Chou et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2012).

Another kind of technology to enhance the emission quantum efficiency based on the assembly-induced emission of CNCs is to control the assembly of CNCs, such as layer-by-layer assembly, evaporation-induced assembly, coassembly with anionic or neutral polymers, and so on (Podsiadlo et al., 2005; Majoinen, Kontturi, Ikkala, & Gray, 2012; Dumanli et al., 2014; Bardet, Belgacem, & Bras, 2015). Unfortunately, those works mainly focused on a chiral assembling structure, which results in iridescence and lead to reading information wrongly. The chiral structure comes from the electric repulsion between CNC particles, which usually adsorb intensive charges due to their high aspect ratio (Xiang et al., 2019; Li, Wang, Long, Gan, & Huang, 2019). Although the chirality could be weakened by introducing external force fields of gravity or capillary into assembly process, the force fields loosened the CNC arrays and made them brittle. Moreover, the chirality could not be removed completely, and the EQY was still low (Diaz, Wu, Martini, Youngblood, & Moon, 2013; Gan et al., 2019). Thus, the control on the CNC assembling process should be focused on assembling CNC closely in a nanoscale environment.

Herein, we established a new strategy to control the assembly structure of CNC in the nanoscale space of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and integrate emission-enhancement technologies. Via quantitatively adjusting the chemical surface structure of CNC, the particle number of CNC assembled in the MOF of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) decreased to a single-digit level. Both the emission enhancement from the nanoscale assembly and decrease from the high refractive index of ZIF-8 were studied. Carbon nanodots, acting as conductive nanoparticles and inducing plasmonic resonance enhancement, were then introduced into MOF to remove that decrease effect as shown in Scheme 1 . The EQY was further controlled by adjusting the plasmonic resonance wavelength of CND, which should be close to the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the assembly-induced emission of CNC. It is hypothesized that such a strategy can help design new assembly structures of cellulose-based nanomaterials with an improved EQY, and the carbohydrate material should have great potential in hiding optical information.

Cotton linter was supplied by Hubei Chemical Fiber Group Co., Ltd. (China). Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AR), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, AR) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, GR) were purchased from Chengdu Keshi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96.0 %, granular) and methyl alcohol (CH3OH, AR) Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Industry (Group) Co., Ltd. 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, 98 %) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry. Urea was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China).

The Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) was extracted by a H2SO4-hydrolysis method. Firstly, 25 g cotton linters were immersed into a 1 L 2 wt% aqueous NaOH solution for 12 h under stirring at room temperature. The solution was washed to neutral, and stirred at 45 ℃ for 60 min in a 250 mL 65 % H2SO4 to remove the amorphous region from cellulose fibers. The obtained suspension was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged (4000 rpm, 5 min) for at least three times with distilled water to neutral. To obtain solid-state CNCs, the suspension needed to be dialyzed for 5 d and lyophilized.

0.893 g (3.00 mmol) Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.985 g (12.0 mmol) 2-methylimidazole were immersed into 10.0 mL and 15.0 mL methyl alcohol respectively. After being solved completely, 2-methylimidazole was added into Zn(NO3)2·6H2O slowly, and the mixture was stirred at 45 ℃ 600 rpm for 30 min. Before washing the sediment with methyl alcohol, the obtained suspension was placed for 2 h to steady the crystals. After centrifuging (10,000 rpm, 5 min) for three times, the obtained ZIF-8 was placed into vacuum drying chamber at 60 ℃ for 24 h.

0.490 g CNC obtained from above method and 0.500 g (6.09 mmol) 2-methylimidazole were solved into 15.0 mL methyl alcohol and then ultrasonically mixed for 2 min at 60 %. Meanwhile, 0.485 g (5.91 mmol) 2-methylimidazole and 0.893 g (3.00 mmol) Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were immersed into 10.0 mL methyl alcohol and stirred until clear. Then, adding the second mixture slowly into the first one and reacting for 2 h, after the reaction completed, the mixture was placed for 2 h to steady the crystallization. Then centrifuging (12,000 rpm, 5 min) for three times and drying in vacuum drying chamber at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

A mixture of carbamide, citric acid, and distilled water with different ratio were sealed in a 25.0 mL Para-polyphenol-liner autoclave, and heating at 150 ℃ for 7 h. Then cooling to room temperature, and dialyzing for 3 day (The reactants ratios to obtain the samples of CNDX, where X is a code, are shown in Table S1).

0.10 g ZIF-8@CNC was immersed into 10.0 mL above CND solution (0.15 wt%), stirring for 15 h at 45 ℃. Then, the suspension was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min) for three times and dried in vacuum drying chamber at 37 ℃ for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of CNC, CND, ZIF-8@CNC and CND@ZIF-8@CNC

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CNC and ZIF-8 coated CNC (ZIF-8@CNC) were observed by Atomic Force Microscope (Icon dimension; Bruker, Germany) in Scanasyst-Air mode. The appropriate concentration of CNC and ZIF-8@CNC suspension were dropped on the Highly-Oriented Pyrolytic Graphite substrates and dried for measurement. The dimensions of samples were estimated from 100 samples by the associated software.

TEM was used to observe the morphology of Carbon nanodots (CND) and CND decorated ZIF-8@CNC (CND@ZIF-8@CNC) by Talos F200X (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Czech). The appropriate concentration of CND and CND@ZIF-8@CNC suspension were dropped on the Carbon support film and dried for measurement.

XRD was used to study the structure property of ZIF-8@CNC, and it was carried out on X-Ray diffractometer (XRD-6000; Shimadzu, Japan). Cu Kα radiation was operated at λ = 0.154 nm from 3° to 40° with a step speed of 2°/min.
The Fourier infrared spectroscopy analysis of CNC, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8@CNC was conducted on Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Madison, WI 53711, USA, 4000−400 cm−1).
The X-Ray photoelectron analysis was conducted on a VG Multilab 2000X (Thermal Electron, USA) to study the change of binding energy between CNC, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8@CNC.
The contents of ZIF-8@CNC and CNC was studied by Chemical Components Analysis (ELEMENTAR VARIO MICRO SELECT, Elementar Analysensysteme, Germany).

The photoluminescence intensity of ZIF-8@CNC, CNC, ZIF-8, CND, CND@ZIF-8, CND@CNC and CND@ZIF-8@CNC was conducted on a 5JI-004 Photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-technologies Corporation, Japan) in Luminescence mode. All the samples were tested in powder form. The scan speed of each sample was 240 nm/min, and the excitation slit and emission slit are 2.5 nm.

The emission quantum yield of CND@ZIF-8@CNC was studied by a FLS980 Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). A blank sample (Empty quartz cell) was scanned to obtain the photoluminescence spectra for reference, and then CND@ZIF-8@CNC was sealed in the sample cell and scanned. The data was analyzed by the software of FLS980. All the samples were tested in powder form and the emission quantum yield (EQY) can be calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
E O Y =
B
2
/
(
A
1
−
B
1
)
where A 1 is the integrals of the excitation region of blank sample, A 2 is the integrals of the emission region of blank sample, B 1 is the integrals of the excitation region of CND@ZIF-8@CNC, and B 2 is the integrals of the emission region of CND@ZIF-8@CNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assemble cellulose nanocrystals in the nanoscale space of metal-organic frameworks
ce:section.ce:para: The diameter/length of CNC increased from 0.034 to 0.07 after CNC reacted with ZIF-8 (ZIF-8@CNC), as shown in Fig. 1 , which suggested that CNC was assembled in the nano-environment of ZIF-8. The assembly should be ascribed to the change in surface charge, as shown in Fig. 2 . The surface charge of CNC decreased from −38.7 eV to −11.0 eV after it reacted with ZIF-8, weakening the electrostatic repulsion between CNC particles and promoting their assembly. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) also showed that the ZIF-8@CNC presented strong diffractions at 7.4°, 10.4°, 12.7°, and 18.0°, should be attributed to the (110), (200), (211), and (222) facets of ZIF-8 (Fig. 3 a), respectively (Guan et al., 2017). Those facets came from the face-centered-cube crystal structure of ZIF-8, which was consistent with change in the cross profile of CNC particles from rod-like to cuboid-like (Fig. S1). Those results indicated that CNCs were successfully introduced into the nanoscale environment of ZIF-8.

The interaction between ZIF-8 and CNCs in the ZIF-8@CNC was studied by Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FT-IR spectrum of ZIF-8 showed a sharp peak of -C-N bonds at 1144 cm−1, which shifted to a high-frequency of 1164 cm−1 in ZIF-8@CNC (Fig. 3b). The broad adsorption of CNC at 3344 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups, showed a high-frequency shift to 3410 cm−1 in ZIF-8@CNC (Fig. 3b), indicating that the H-bonds between hydroxyl groups of CNC were weakened. This feature suggested that the -C-N bond in ZIF-8 formed H-bonds with the hydroxyl group of CNC (Zhu et al., 2018). Furthermore, the XPS peaks of oxygen in the hydroxyl groups of CNC at 531.3 eV shifted to a higher energy level of 533.1 eV in ZIF-8@CNC.The peaks at 399.0 eV and 400.5 eV, which ascribed to the -C-N-C bond and the -N-C = N bond in the imidazole group of ZIF-8, also shifted to a higher energy level of 399.6 eV and 400.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c and 3d) (Wei, Li, & Zhang, 2017). Such three shifts confirmed the H-bonds between ZIF-8 and CNC.

To study the assembly structure of CNC in the nanoscale environment of ZIF-8 quantitatively, the following equations were used to calculate the average number of CNCs assembling in the ZIF-8@CNC.
(2) m = ρV
(3) mt = ρzVx + ρcV(1 - x)
(4)
V = L
( 0.25 π
D
2
)
where m t is the mass of ZIF-8@CNC, ρ z is the density of ZIF-8, ρ c is the density of CNC, V is the volume of ZIF-8@CNC, x is the volume ratio of ZIF-8@CNC, L is the length of ZIF-8@CNC, and D is diameter of ZIF-8@CNC. The density of CNC was 1.56 × 106 g·m−3, and the density of ZIF-8 was 9.5 × 105 g·m−3 (Park et al., 2006; Paralikara, Simonsen, & Lombardi, 2008). Since
(5)
m z
=
ρ z
V x
where m z is the mass of ZIF-8, we then obtained the mass ratio of ZIF-8 in ZIF-8@CNC (ω ZIF-8) as Eq. (6):
(6)
ω
Z I F - 8
=
m z
m t
=
ρ z
V x
ρ z
V x +
ρ c
V
1 - x
where the nitrogen content of ZIF-8@CNC, obtained from elemental analysis, was 1.04 %, and the theorical nitrogen content of ZIF-8 was 25 %, so the value of ω ZIF-8 should be 0.0416 and the value of x was 0.066.

According to the data collected from atomic force microscopy (AFM) images, we found that the average volume of ZIF-8@CNC, which the ω ZIF-8 was 0.0416, was 1.03 × 10−23 m3. Then, the volume of CNC in ZIF-8@CNC could be calculated by Eq. (7):
(7)
V c
= V
( 1 − x )
since the volume of CNC was about 2.24 × 10−24 m3 (from AFM image of Fig. 2a), a ZIF-8@CNC particle should contain about 4 CNC particles.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of ZIF-8@CNC proved the number of CNC in ZIF-8 (Fig. S2 and S3). However, the low conductivity of ZIF-8@CNC made the particles easily melt under the electron beam, and only ZIF-8 (when CNCs could not) could maintained their morphology.

Fortunately, two facts could prove the increase in achiral degree of CNC assembly that dominated the intensity of assembly-induced emission. Firstly, the decrease in surface charge should lead to a decrease in chiral degree of CNC assembly (Beck-Candanedo, Roman, & Gray, 2005; Shafiei-Sabet, Hamad, & Hatzikiriakos, 2013; Honorato-Rios et al., 2016). Secondly, the length/diameter ratio (L/D) of ZIF@CNC was 16.7, which meant the minimal orientation degree (OD min, proportional to achiral degree) should be 0.999, which was calculated by Eq. (8):
(8)
O D m i n
=
2 3
1 +
cos 2
a r c t a n (
n
L / D )
- 1
2

Considering the orientation degree of CNC assembly without ZIF-8 should be 0.993 (Equation S1) (Gan et al., 2019), we believed that achiral degree of CNC was improved in the nano-environment of ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhance the photonic emission of CNC assembly
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , after assembling in ZIF-8, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of CNC increased by about 50 %, compared to CNCs assembling via solvents vaporing (Fig. 4a and 4c). To study the mechanism of the photoluminescence phenomenon, we tested the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of ZIF-8 and carbon nanodots (CNDs) in solid state. Since the CND may suffer the aggregation induced quenching, we diffused CNDs into ZIF-8 pores. As shown in Fig. 4a and 4b, both ZIF-8 and the CND presented weak PL intensity in solid state, and the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of CND@ZIF-8@CNC were equal to those of unmodified CNC. This result indicated that this increase must come from the nanoscale assembly structure of CNC.

Via further modifying CNDs into the ZIF-8@CNC system (Fig. S4), the intensity of CND@ZIF-8@CNC enhanced remarkably, and the emission quantum yield (EQY) of CND@ZIF-8@CNC reached 64.84 % (from 9.37 % of CNC), much higher than that of the CNC arrays directly from evaporation-induced assembly (13.4 %) (Gan et al., 2019). Such a 6.9-time increase in EQY made the CND@ZIF-8@CNC suspension capable for invisible inks, as shown in Fig. 5 . Notably, the photoluminescent intensity of inks with CNC only was not strong enough for the hidden information to be easily read under ultraviolet light (Fig. S5).

The increase in EQY should come from the plasmonic resonance of CND, and we confirmed it first by change the excitation and emission wavelength of CND. As shown in Fig. 6 , only when both of excitation and emission wavelength of CND were close to those of CNC assembly (here was the CND4), the photoluminescent intensity could reach the maximum. This result was consistent with the phenomenon of plasmonic resonance enhancement. Calculating the radiative recombination rate enhancement factor (F) with the following equations:
(9)
F =
1
τ *
1 τ
= τ /
τ *
(10)
E R =
(
P L I
C N C @ Z I F − 8 @ C N C
/
P L I
C N C
)
/
P L I
Z I F − 8 @ C N C
where the τ is the original lifetime of PL emission (4.63 ns), τ* is the enhanced lifetime of PL emission after CND was introduced (1.12 ns), ER is the actual enhanced ratio of CND@ZIF-8@CNC, PLI CNC@ZIF-8@CNC is the PL intensity of CND@ZIF-8@CNC, PLI CNC is the PL intensity of CNC, and PLI ZIF-8@CNC is the PL intensity of ZIF-8@CNC.

The calculated value of F was 4.13, which was close to the actual value of the enhancement ratio (5.5 times from Eq. (10), Fig. 4b and 4c), indicating that the PL enhancement of CND@ZIF-8@CNC could be ascribed to the coupling of CND and CNC. Meanwhile, the photoluminescent intensity of CND@CNC, in which CNC contacted CND directly, and without ZIF-8 was much lower than that of CNC. Such a photoluminescence quench indicated that the CND exerted a plasmonic effect on CNC, because plasmonic could enhanced photoluminescence only if there was an isolation layer, which was ZIF-8 in CND@ZIF-8@CNC.

Furthermore, based on the relationships among PL lifetime (τ), EQY, and recombination rate were also confirmed with the Eqs. (11), (12), (13), and (14) to study the theoretically maximal emission quantum yield increased via plasmonic resonance (EQY*max).
(11)
E Q Y
*

=

k

k
n A I E

s p
where the k AIE is the recombination rate of aggregation-induced emission, k nAIE is the recombination rate of non-aggregation-induced emission, and k sp is the coupling rate of the exciton-located surface plasmon. The C ext is the light extraction efficiency, which could be assumed to be 100 % (Okamoto et al., 2005). Since
(12)

where τ CNC and τ* are the PL lifetime of CNC (4.63 ns) and CND@ZIF-8@CNC (1.12 ns), respectively. Then we could get the Eq. (15):
(15)

Then we found the EQY*max should be 78.07 %, which was different from the actual value of 64.84 % (Calculated by Fig. S6). The difference should be attributed to the extinction of light by CND@ZIF-8@CNC nanoparticles.

The attenuation of the light intensity through medium can be described by Beer’s law：
(16)
I
( x )
=
I
0
e
− a x
where I(x) is intensity of light at distance of x through the medium, I 0 is the original intensity of light, and α is attenuation coefficient of the medium.

Since the medium here were the solid ZIF-8 nanoparticles, the scattering of the material should be considered, so the attenuation coefficient was the sum of absorption coefficient and scattering coefficient:
(17)
a =
k
a b
+
k
s c
where k ab is the absorption coefficient, and k sc is the scattering coefficient.

To acquire the absorption coefficient and the scattering coefficient, ultraviolet spectrophotometry was used to test the material in the dispersion of water and at the solid state (Fig. S7). The absorption coefficient of the nanoparticles can be calculated via Lambert-Beer law.
(18)
A = a b c
where A is absorbance of the nanoparticle in the dispersion, b is the thickness of the solution layer, and c is the concentration of solution.

The calculation of scattering coefficient followed the Kubelka-Munk Theory:
(19)
F ( R ∞ )
=
k a b / k s c
=
1 -
R ∞
2
2
R ∞
where F(R ∞) is the Kubelka-Munk function, and R ∞ is the absolute index of refraction. Through analyzing the data of ultraviolet spectrophotometry, the k ab of CND@ZIF-8@CNC was found to be 1.729 × 10−3 m−1, and k ab/k sc was 0.022, so the k sc was 3.241 × 10−1 m−1. From the Eq. (15), α of CND@ZIF-8@CNC was obtained to be 3.243 × 10−1 m−1.

To study the distance of attenuation, we needed to know the actual height of CND@ZIF-8@CNC during the emission quantum yield test. The height of CND@ZIF-8@CNC could be described as:
(20)
H n
=
H m
×
ρ b
ρ n
where H n is the height of CND@ZIF-8@CNC nanoparticles, H m is the height of the test mold, which is 1.0 × 10−3 m, ρ b is the bulk density of CND@ZIF-8@CNC nanoparticles, and ρ n is the actual density.

Since the content of CND was extremely low and could be ignored, the actual density of CND@ZIF-8@CNC can be explained as:
(21)
ρ n = ω CNC ρ c + ω ZIF-8 ρ z
(22)
ω
C N C
=
1 − ω
Z I F − 8
where ω CNC is the mass ratio of CNC in ZIF-8@CNC, so the ρ n was 1.535 × 106 g∙m−3. ρ b here was 4.290 × 105 g∙m−3, and thus the H n was 2.795 × 10−4 m.

Via putting the data into Eq. (16), the worst attenuation of CND@ZIF-8@CNC was 40.40 %. Because the attenuation of light is an exponential decay, we can use integral operation to get the attenuation value on average:
(23)
A t
=
∫ 0
H n
e
- α x
d x
(24)
A n
=
A t
H n
(25)
E Q Y
min
=
E Q Y
C N C
×
( E Q Y / E Q Y * )
×
A n
where A t is the total attenuation of CND@ZIF-8@CNC, A n is the average attenuation of it, which was 65.76 %. Then we obtained the theoretically minimal emission quantum yield (EQY*min) of CND@ZIF-8@CNC with Eq. (26), in which the extinction effect was maximized.
(26)
EQY* min = EQY*max × A n
This result of EQY min was 51.34 %, indicating that the EQY of CND@ZIF-8@CNC should be in the range from 51.34 %–78.07 %, which was exactly consistent with the experimental data (64.84 %).

In summary, we successfully established an assemble-induced emission composite with high emission quantum yield. In this composite, about 4 particles of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were assembled closely in the nanoscale environment of metal organic frameworks (ZIF-8) leading to a primary 50 % increase in emission intensity. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were introduced into the ZIF-8@CNC to remove the decrease in induced photo intensity from the extinction of ZIF-8, which also took the advantages of CND as a conductive nanoparticle with plasmonic enhancement. Such a plasmonic effect further increased the emission intensity by 5.5 times when the plasmonic wavelength was close to the diffraction wavelength of CNC assembly, and the final quantum yield of CNC reached to 64.84 %. Such a solid-state emission materials can be used to prepare invisible inks, and since they also maintained the high chemical modification ability and biocompatibility, they should also be of great potential in optical pesticide tracking and green catalysis.

Shuyu Zheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Siyuan Liu: Resources, Investigation. Bo Xiao: Resources, Investigation. Liu Liu: Investigation. Xinjie Wan: Investigation. Yanbin Gong: Validation, Data curation. Siqi Wei: Investigation. Chenglong Luo: Investigation. Lin Gan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Jin Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973175), the Project for Chongqing University Innovation Research Group of Chongqing Education Committee (CXQT19008), the Chongqing Talent Plan for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Demonstration Team (CQYC201903243), and the S&T Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Bishan District in Chongqing (Bskj20200011).
